revision.text:
[[Image:Guji0148.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Kannushi]] A Kannushi ({{lang|ja|神主}}) is responsible for maintenance of a [[Shinto]] [[shrine]] (jinja) as well as leading worship. He generally does not proselytize. Traditionally, most jinja did not have a Kannushi and were maintained by a committee of local populace who are called Ujiko ({{lang|ja|氏子}}).
[[Category:Shinto shrines|*]]

[[Image:Guji0148.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Kannushi]] [[Image:ShintoistPriest.PNG|thumb|right|Kannushi]] A Kannushi ({{lang|ja|神主}}) is responsible for maintenance of a [[Shinto]] [[shrine]] (jinja) as well as leading worship. He generally does not proselytize. Traditionally, most jinja did not have a Kannushi and were maintained by a committee of local populace who are called Ujiko ({{lang|ja|氏子}}).
[[Category:Religious occupations]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]

He generally does not proselytize. Traditionally, most jinja did not have a Kannushi and were maintained by a committee of local populace who are called Ujiko ({{lang|ja|氏子}}).

[[ja:神職]]

{{Unreferenced|date=June 2008}} [[Image:Guji0148.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Kannushi in front of the haiden.]] [[Image:ShintoistPriest.PNG|thumb|right|Young Kannushi from [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū]].]] A '''''kannushi''''' ({{lang|ja|神主}}) is responsible for the maintenance of a [[Shinto]] [[shinto shrine|shrine]] (''jinja'') as well as leading worship. He generally does not proselytize. Traditionally, most jinja did not have a kannushi and were maintained by a committee of the local populace who are called ''ujiko'' ({{lang|ja|氏子}}).
The Kannushi's [[vestments]] are mostly of the [[Heian Period]] style which was influenced by [[Tang Dynasty]] [[Hanfu|Chinese dress]].
==See also== * [[Miko]]
[[Category:Religious occupations]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese religions terms]]
{{japan-reli-stub}}
[[ja:神職]]

{{Unreferenced|date=June 2008}} {{Expand Japanese|神職|topic=culture|date=March 2009}} [[Image:Guji0148.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Kannushi in front of the haiden.]]
A {{nihongo|'''''kannushi'''''|神主}}) or {{nihongo|'''shinshoku'''|神職}} is the person responsible for the maintenance of a [[Shinto]] [[shinto shrine|shrine]] (''jinja'') as well as for leading worship. He generally does not proselytize. Traditionally, most shrines did not have a ''kannushi'' and were maintained by a committee of the local populace who are called {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}}.
The ''kannushi'''s [[vestments]] are mostly of the [[Heian Period]] style, which was influenced by [[Tang Dynasty]] [[Hanfu|Chinese dress]].
==See also== * [[Miko]]
[[Category:Religious occupations]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese religions terms]]
{{japan-reli-stub}}

He generally does not proselytize. Traditionally, most shrines did not have a ''kannushi'' and were maintained by a committee of the local populace who are called {{nihongo|''ujiko''|氏子}}.

revision.comment: Rewritten article with references, deleted unreferenced material.
revision.text:
[[Image:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|thumb|250px|right|A ''kannushi'']]A {{nihongo|'''kannushi'''|神主}} (originally pronounced ''kamunushi'') (also called {{nihongo|'''shinshoku'''|神職}} is the person responsible for the maintenance of a [[Shinto]] [[shinto shrine|shrine]] (''jinja'') as well as for leading worship of a certain ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for ''kannushi'' are sometimes also read ''jinshu'' with the same meaning.
Originally a ''kannushi'' was a man capable of miracles or a holy man dedicated to ''[[kami]]'' worhip, but later the term evolved to being synonymous with ''shinshoku'', that is, a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="ya">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/|title=Kannushi|last=Moriyasu|language=Japanese|first=Jin|work=Nihon Hyakka Zensho|publisher=Shogakukan|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> The word can still can be used to mean the chief of the ''shinshoku''.<ref name="IK"/>
In ancient times, because of the coincidence of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who lead the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The ''kannushi'' was an intermediary between ''kami'' and could transmit his will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> The term appears in both [[Kojiki]] (680 AD) and [[Nihon Shoki]] (720 AD).<ref name="ya"/> In them, Empress Jungū and Emperor Suijin respectively become ''kannushi''.<ref name="eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285|title=Kannushi|last=Nishimuta|first=Takao|date=2007/3/28|work=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> Within the same shrine, for example at [[Ise Jingū]] or [[Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of ''kannushi'' at the same time, for example {{nihongo|''Ō-kannushi''|大神主}}, the {{nihongo|''Sō-kannushi''|総神主}}, or {{nihongo|''Gon-kannushi''|権神主}}.<ref name="ya"/><ref name="eos"/> ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their work by women called ''miko''.
==References==
<references/>
==See also== * [[Miko]]
[[Category:Religious occupations]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese religions terms]]
[[ja:神職]]
{{japan-reli-stub}}

Originally a ''kannushi'' was a man capable of miracles or a holy man dedicated to ''[[kami]]'' worhip, but later the term evolved to being synonymous with ''shinshoku'', that is, a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="ya">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/|title=Kannushi|last=Moriyasu|language=Japanese|first=Jin|work=Nihon Hyakka Zensho|publisher=Shogakukan|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> The word can still can be used to mean the chief of the ''shinshoku''.<ref name="IK"/>
In ancient times, because of the coincidence of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who lead the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The ''kannushi'' was an intermediary between ''kami'' and could transmit his will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> The term appears in both [[Kojiki]] (680 AD) and [[Nihon Shoki]] (720 AD).<ref name="ya"/> In them, Empress Jungū and Emperor Suijin respectively become ''kannushi''.<ref name="eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285|title=Kannushi|last=Nishimuta|first=Takao|date=2007/3/28|work=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> Within the same shrine, for example at [[Ise Jingū]] or [[Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of ''kannushi'' at the same time, for example {{nihongo|''Ō-kannushi''|大神主}}, the {{nihongo|''Sō-kannushi''|総神主}}, or {{nihongo|''Gon-kannushi''|権神主}}.<ref name="ya"/><ref name="eos"/> ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
==See also== * [[Miko]]
==References==
<references/>

[[Image:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|thumb|250px|right|A ''kannushi'']]A {{nihongo|'''kannushi'''|神主}} (originally pronounced ''kamunushi'') (also called {{nihongo|'''shinshoku'''|神職}} is the person responsible for the maintenance of a [[Shinto]] [[shinto shrine|shrine]] (''jinja'') as well as for leading worship of a certain ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for ''kannushi'' are sometimes also read ''jinshu'' with the same meaning.
Originally a ''kannushi'' was a man capable of miracles or a holy man dedicated to ''[[kami]]'' worhip, but later the term evolved to being synonymous with ''shinshoku'', that is, a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="ya">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/|title=Kannushi|last=Moriyasu|language=Japanese|first=Jin|work=Nihon Hyakka Zensho|publisher=Shogakukan|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref><ref name="IK"/>
In ancient times, because of the coincidence of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who lead the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The ''kannushi'' was an intermediary between ''kami'' and could transmit his will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> The term appears in both [[Kojiki]] (680 AD) and [[Nihon Shoki]] (720 AD).<ref name="ya"/> In them, Empress Jungū and Emperor Suijin respectively become ''kannushi''.<ref name="eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285|title=Kannushi|last=Nishimuta|first=Takao|date=2007/3/28|work=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> Within the same shrine, for example at [[Ise Jingū]] or [[Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of ''kannushi'' at the same time called for example {{nihongo|''Ō-kannushi''|大神主}}, {{nihongo|''Sō-kannushi''|総神主}}, or {{nihongo|''Gon-kannushi''|権神主}}.<ref name="ya"/><ref name="eos"/> ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
==See also== * [[Miko]]

[[ja:神職]] {{japan-reli-stub}}

[[Image:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|thumb|250px|right|A ''kannushi'']]A {{nihongo|'''kannushi'''|神主|god master}} (originally pronounced ''kamunushi'') (also called {{nihongo|'''shinshoku'''|神職}} is the person responsible for the maintenance of a [[Shinto]] [[shinto shrine|shrine]] (''jinja'') as well as for leading worship of a certain ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for ''kannushi'' are sometimes also read ''jinshu'' with the same meaning.
Originally a ''kannushi'' was a man capable of miracles or a holy man dedicated to ''[[kami]]'' worhip, but later the term evolved to being synonymous with ''shinshoku'', that is, a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="ya">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/|title=Kannushi|last=Moriyasu|language=Japanese|first=Jin|work=Nihon Hyakka Zensho|publisher=Shogakukan|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref><ref name="IK"/>
In ancient times, because of the coincidence of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who lead the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The ''kannushi'' was an intermediary between ''kami'' and could transmit his will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> The term appears in both [[Kojiki]] (680 AD) and [[Nihon Shoki]] (720 AD).<ref name="ya"/> In them, Empress Jungū and Emperor Suijin respectively become ''kannushi''.<ref name="eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285|title=Kannushi|last=Nishimuta|first=Takao|date=2007/3/28|work=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> Within the same shrine, for example at [[Ise Jingū]] or [[Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of ''kannushi'' at the same time called for example {{nihongo|''Ō-kannushi''|大神主}}, {{nihongo|''Sō-kannushi''|総神主}}, or {{nihongo|''Gon-kannushi''|権神主}}.<ref name="ya"/><ref name="eos"/> ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
''Kannushi'' marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi">{{cite book|last=Nishimura|first=Hajime|title=A Comparative History of Ideas|publisher=Motilal Banarsidass|date=1998|isbn=978-8120810044}}</ref> The dresses worn by ''kannushi'' are not religious garb, but simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/>
==See also== * [[Miko]]

[[Image:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|thumb|250px|right|A ''kannushi'']]A {{nihongo|'''kannushi'''|神主|god master}} (originally pronounced ''kamunushi'') (also called {{nihongo|'''shinshoku'''|神職}} is the person responsible for the maintenance of a [[Shinto]] [[shinto shrine|shrine]] (''jinja'') as well as for leading worship of a certain ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for ''kannushi'' are sometimes also read ''jinshu'' with the same meaning.
Originally a ''kannushi'' was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of the practice of purificatory rites, was capable to work as a medium for a ''[[kami]]'', but later the term evolved to being synonymous with ''shinshoku'', that is, a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="ya">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/|title=Kannushi|last=Moriyasu|language=Japanese|first=Jin|work=Nihon Hyakka Zensho|publisher=Shogakukan|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref><ref name="IK"/>
In ancient times, because of the coincidence of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who lead the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The ''kannushi'' was an intermediary between ''kami'' and could transmit his will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> The term appears in both [[Kojiki]] (680 AD) and [[Nihon Shoki]] (720 AD).<ref name="ya"/> In them, Empress Jungū and Emperor Suijin respectively become ''kannushi''.<ref name="eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285|title=Kannushi|last=Nishimuta|first=Takao|date=2007/3/28|work=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> Within the same shrine, for example at [[Ise Jingū]] or [[Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of ''kannushi'' at the same time called for example {{nihongo|''Ō-kannushi''|大神主}}, {{nihongo|''Sō-kannushi''|総神主}}, or {{nihongo|''Gon-kannushi''|権神主}}.<ref name="ya"/><ref name="eos"/> ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
[[File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|thumb|left|A ''kannushi'' with a ''miko'']]''Kannushi'' marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi">{{cite book|last=Nishimura|first=Hajime|title=A Comparative History of Ideas|publisher=Motilal Banarsidass|date=1998|isbn=978-8120810044}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally mandated, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB">{{cite web|url=http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku|title=Shinshoku|publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica Online|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> The dresses worn by ''kannushi'' are not religious garb, but simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/>
To become a ''kannushi'' a novice must study at a university approved by the [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honchō] (Association of Shintō Shrines), for example [[Tokyo]]'s [[Kokugakuin University]].<ref name="EB"/> Women can become ''kannushi'' and succeed their husband in their job in case of death.<ref name="EB"/> ==See also== * [[Miko]]

The term appears in both [[Kojiki]] (680 AD) and [[Nihon Shoki]] (720 AD).<ref name="ya"/> In them, Empress Jungū and Emperor Suijin respectively become ''kannushi''.<ref name="eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285|title=Kannushi|last=Nishimuta|first=Takao|date=2007/3/28|work=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> Within the same shrine, for example at [[Ise Jingū]] or [[Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of ''kannushi'' at the same time called for example {{nihongo|''Ō-kannushi''|大神主}}, {{nihongo|''Sō-kannushi''|総神主}}, or {{nihongo|''Gon-kannushi''|権神主}}.<ref name="ya"/><ref name="eos"/> ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
[[File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|thumb|left|A ''kannushi'' with a ''miko'']]''Kannushi'' marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi">{{cite book|last=Nishimura|first=Hajime|title=A Comparative History of Ideas|publisher=Motilal Banarsidass|date=1998|isbn=978-8120810044}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally mandated, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB">{{cite web|url=http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku|title=Shinshoku|publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica Online|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> The dresses worn by ''kannushi'' are not religious garb, but simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/>

The term appears in both [[Kojiki]] (680 AD) and [[Nihon Shoki]] (720 AD).<ref name="ya"/> In them, Empress Jungū and Emperor Suijin respectively become ''kannushi''.<ref name="eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285|title=Kannushi|last=Nishimuta|first=Takao|date=2007/3/28|work=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> Within the same shrine, for example at [[Ise Jingū]] or [[Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of ''kannushi'' at the same time called for example {{nihongo|''Ō-kannushi''|大神主}}, {{nihongo|''Sō-kannushi''|総神主}}, or {{nihongo|''Gon-kannushi''|権神主}}.<ref name="ya"/><ref name="eos"/> ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
[[File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|thumb|left|A ''kannushi'' with a ''miko'']]''Kannushi'' marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi">{{cite book|last=Nishimura|first=Hajime|title=A Comparative History of Ideas|publisher=Motilal Banarsidass|date=1998|isbn=978-8120810044}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB">{{cite web|url=http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku|title=Shinshoku|publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica Online|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> The dresses worn by ''kannushi'' do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/>

==History== Originally the ''kannushi'' was an intermediary between ''kami'' and could transmit his will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A ''kannushi'' was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of the practice of purificatory rites, was capable to work as a medium for a ''[[kami]]'', but later the term evolved to being synonymous with ''shinshoku'', that is, a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="ya">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/|title=Kannushi|last=Moriyasu|language=Japanese|first=Jin|work=Nihon Hyakka Zensho|publisher=Shogakukan|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref><ref name="IK"/>
In ancient times, because of the coincidence of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who lead the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both [[Kojiki]] (680 AD) and [[Nihon Shoki]] (720 AD).<ref name="ya"/> In them, Empress Jungū and Emperor Suijin respectively become ''kannushi''.<ref name="eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285|title=Kannushi|last=Nishimuta|first=Takao|date=2007/3/28|work=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> Within the same shrine, for example at [[Ise Jingū]] or [[Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of ''kannushi'' at the same time called for example {{nihongo|''Ō-kannushi''|大神主}}, {{nihongo|''Sō-kannushi''|総神主}}, or {{nihongo|''Gon-kannushi''|権神主}}.<ref name="ya"/><ref name="eos"/>
[[File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|thumb|left|A ''kannushi'' with a ''miko'']]''Kannushi'' can marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi">{{cite book|last=Nishimura|first=Hajime|title=A Comparative History of Ideas|publisher=Motilal Banarsidass|date=1998|isbn=978-8120810044}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB">{{cite web|url=http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku|title=Shinshoku|publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica Online|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> The clothes they wear, for example the [[jōe]], do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detaill reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honchō] (Association of Shintō Shrines), typically [[Tokyo]]'s [[Kokugakuin University]].<ref name="EB"/> Women can become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
==See also== * [[Miko]]

[[Image:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|thumb|250px|right|A ''kannushi'']]A {{nihongo|'''kannushi'''|神主|god master}} (originally pronounced ''kamunushi'') (also called {{nihongo|'''shinshoku'''|神職}}) is the person responsible for the maintenance of a [[Shinto]] [[shinto shrine|shrine]] (''jinja'') as well as for leading worship of a certain ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for ''kannushi'' are sometimes also read ''jinshu'' with the same meaning.

[[File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|thumb|left|A ''kannushi'' with a ''miko'']]''Kannushi'' can marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi">{{cite book|last=Nishimura|first=Hajime|title=A Comparative History of Ideas|publisher=Motilal Banarsidass|date=1998|isbn=978-8120810044}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB">{{cite web|url=http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku|title=Shinshoku|publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica Online|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> The clothes they wear, for example the [[jōe]], do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detaill reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.

[[File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|thumb|left|A ''kannushi'' wearing a ''[[jōe]]'' accompanied by a ''miko'']]''Kannushi'' can marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi">{{cite book|last=Nishimura|first=Hajime|title=A Comparative History of Ideas|publisher=Motilal Banarsidass|date=1998|isbn=978-8120810044}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB">{{cite web|url=http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku|title=Shinshoku|publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica Online|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> The clothes they wear, for example the ''[[jōe]]'', the ''ebōshi'' and the ''kariginu'' (see photos), do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by ''kannushi'' include a baton called ''[[Shaku (Japanese ritual baton)|shaku]]'' and a wand decorated with white paper streamers (''[[shide]]'') called ''[[ōnusa]]''. ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honchō] (Association of Shintō Shrines), typically [[Tokyo]]'s [[Kokugakuin University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> Women can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>

Originally the ''kannushi'' was an intermediary between ''kami'' and could transmit his will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A ''kannushi'' was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of the practice of purificatory rites, was capable to work as a medium for a ''[[kami]]'', but later the term evolved to being synonymous with ''shinshoku'', that is, a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/|title=Kannushi|last=Moriyasu|language=Japanese|first=Jin|work=Nihon Hyakka Zensho|publisher=Shogakukan|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the coincidence of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who lead the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both [[Kojiki]] (680 AD) and [[Nihon Shoki]] (720 AD).<ref name="ya"/> In them, Empress Jungū and Emperor Suijin respectively become ''kannushi''.<ref name="eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285|title=Kannushi|last=Nishimuta|first=Takao|date=2007-03-28|work=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> Within the same shrine, for example at [[Ise Jingū]] or [[Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of ''kannushi'' at the same time called for example {{nihongo|''Ō-kannushi''|大神主}}, {{nihongo|''Sō-kannushi''|総神主}}, or {{nihongo|''Gon-kannushi''|権神主}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/>
[[File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|thumb|left|A ''kannushi'' wearing a ''[[jōe]]'' accompanied by a ''miko'']]''Kannushi'' can marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi">{{cite book|last=Nishimura|first=Hajime|title=A Comparative History of Ideas|publisher=Motilal Banarsidass|year=1998|isbn=978-8120810044}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB">{{cite web|url=http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku|title=Shinshoku|publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica Online|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> The clothes they wear, for example the ''[[jōe]]'', the ''ebōshi'' and the ''kariginu'' (see photos), do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by ''kannushi'' include a baton called ''[[Shaku (Japanese ritual baton)|shaku]]'' and a wand decorated with white paper streamers (''[[shide (Shinto)|shide]]'') called ''[[ōnusa]]''. ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honchō] (Association of Shintō Shrines), typically [[Tokyo]]'s [[Kokugakuin University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> Women can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
==See also== * [[Miko]]

''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.

[[File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|thumb|left|A ''kannushi'' wearing a ''[[jōe]]'' accompanied by a ''miko'']]''Kannushi'' can marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi">{{cite book|last=Nishimura|first=Hajime|title=A Comparative History of Ideas|publisher=Motilal Banarsidass|year=1998|isbn=978-8120810044}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB">{{cite web|url=http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku|title=Shinshoku|publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica Online|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> The clothes they wear, for example the ''[[jōe]]'', the ''ebōshi'' and the ''kariginu'' (see photos), do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by ''kannushi'' include a baton called ''[[Shaku (Japanese ritual baton)|shaku]]'' and a wand decorated with white paper streamers (''[[shide (Shinto)|shide]]'') called ''[[ōnusa]]''. ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the [[Jinja Honchō]] (Association of Shintō Shrines), typically [[Tokyo]]’s [[Kokugakuin University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> Women can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>

The term appears in both the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (680 AD) and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (720 AD).<ref name="ya"/> In them respectively, Empress Jungū and Emperor Suijin become ''kannushi''.<ref name="eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285|title=Kannushi|last=Nishimuta|first=Takao|date=2007-03-28|work=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> Within the same shrine, for example at [[Ise Jingū]] or [[Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of ''kannushi'' at the same time called for example {{nihongo|''Ō-kannushi''|大神主}}, {{nihongo|''Sō-kannushi''|総神主}}, or {{nihongo|''Gon-kannushi''|権神主}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/>
[[File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|thumb|left|A ''kannushi'' wearing a ''[[jōe]]'' accompanied by a ''miko'']]''Kannushi'' can marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi">{{cite book|last=Nishimura|first=Hajime|title=A Comparative History of Ideas|publisher=Motilal Banarsidass|year=1998|isbn=978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB">{{cite web|url=http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku|title=Shinshoku|publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica Online|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> The clothes they wear, for example the ''[[jōe]]'', the ''ebōshi'' and the ''kariginu'' (see photos), do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by ''kannushi'' include a baton called ''[[Shaku (Japanese ritual baton)|shaku]]'' and a wand decorated with white paper streamers (''[[shide (Shinto)|shide]]'') called ''[[ōnusa]]''. ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the [[Jinja Honchō]] (Association of Shintō Shrines), typically [[Tokyo]]’s [[Kokugakuin University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> Women can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
==See also== * [[Miko]], female equivalent

{{redirect|Shinshoku|the [[L'Arc-en-Ciel]] song|Shinshoku (Lose Control)}} [[File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|thumb|250px|right|A ''kannushi'' wearing a garment called ''kariginu'' and a hat called ebōshi]]A {{nihongo3|"god master"|神主|'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced ''kamunushi''}}, also called {{nihongo|'''shinshoku'''|神職}} (meaning ''god's employee''), is the person responsible for the maintenance of a [[Shinto]] [[shinto shrine|shrine]] (''jinja'') as well as for leading worship of a given ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for ''kannushi'' are sometimes also read '''''jinshu''''' with the same meaning.
Originally the ''kannushi'' were intermediaries between ''kami'' and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A ''kannushi'' was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of [[purificatory rite]]s, was capable to work as a medium for a ''[[kami]]'', but later the term evolved to being synonymous with ''shinshoku'', that is, a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya">{{cite web|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/|title=Kannushi|last=Moriyasu|language=Japanese|first=Jin|work=Nihon Hyakka Zensho|publisher=Shogakukan|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (680 AD) and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (720 AD).<ref name="ya"/> In them respectively, Empress Jungū and Emperor Suijin become ''kannushi''.<ref name="eos">{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285|title=Kannushi|last=Nishimuta|first=Takao|date=2007-03-28|work=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> Within the same shrine, for example at [[Ise Jingū]] or [[Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of ''kannushi'' at the same time called for example {{nihongo|''Ō-kannushi''|大神主}}, {{nihongo|''Sō-kannushi''|総神主}}, or {{nihongo|''Gon-kannushi''|権神主}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/>
[[File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|thumb|left|A ''kannushi'' wearing a ''[[jōe]]'' accompanied by a ''miko'']]''Kannushi'' can marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi">{{cite book|last=Nishimura|first=Hajime|title=A Comparative History of Ideas|publisher=Motilal Banarsidass|year=1998|isbn=978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB">{{cite web|url=http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku|title=Shinshoku|publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica Online|accessdate=2009-10-16}}</ref> The clothes they wear, for example the ''[[jōe]]'', the ''ebōshi'' and the ''kariginu'' (see photos), do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by ''kannushi'' include a baton called ''[[Shaku (Japanese ritual baton)|shaku]]'' and a wand decorated with white paper streamers (''[[shide (Shinto)|shide]]'') called ''[[ōnusa]]''. ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the [[Jinja Honchō]] (Association of Shintō Shrines), typically [[Tokyo]]’s [[Kokugakuin University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> Women can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
==See also== * [[Miko]], female equivalent

''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the [[Jinja Honchō]] (Association of Shintō Shrines), typically [[Tokyo]]’s [[Kokugakuin University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> Women can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>

To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[Tokyo]]’s [[Kokugakuin University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[Women in Japan|Women]] can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>

In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (680 AD) and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (720 AD).<ref name="ya"/> In them respectively, Empress Jungū and Emperor Suijin become ''kannushi''.<ref name="eos"> {{cite web | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | work= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | accessdate= 2009-10-16 }} </ref>
Within the same shrine, for example at [[Ise Jingū]] or [[Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of ''kannushi'' at the same time called for example {{nihongo|''Ō-kannushi''|大神主}}, {{nihongo|''Sō-kannushi''|総神主}}, or {{nihongo|''Gon-kannushi''|権神主}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/>
[[File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|thumb|left|A ''kannushi'' wearing a ''[[jōe]]'' accompanied by a ''[[miko]]'']]
''Kannushi'' can marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4 }} </ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web | title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | accessdate= 2009-10-16 }} </ref> The clothes they wear, for example the ''[[jōe]]'', the ''ebōshi'' and the ''kariginu'' (see photos), do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by ''kannushi'' include a baton called ''[[Shaku (Japanese ritual baton)|shaku]]'' and a wand decorated with white paper streamers (''[[shide (Shinto)|shide]]'') called ''[[ōnusa]]''. ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[Tokyo]]’s [[Kokugakuin University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[Women in Japan|Women]] can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>

''Kannushi'' can marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4 }} </ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web | title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | accessdate= 2009-10-16 }} </ref> The clothes they wear, for example the ''[[jōe]]'', the ''ebōshi'' and the ''kariginu'' (see photos), do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by ''kannushi'' include a baton called ''[[Shaku (Japanese ritual baton)|shaku]]'' and a wand decorated with white paper streamers (''[[shide (Shinto)|shide]]'') called ''[[ōnusa]]''. ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[Tokyo]]’s [[Kokugakuin University]] or [[Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[Women in Japan|Women]] can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>

Originally the ''kannushi'' were intermediaries between ''kami'' and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A ''kannushi'' was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of [[Harae|purificatory rites]], was able to work as a medium for a [[kami]]. In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (680 AD) and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (720 AD).<ref name="ya"/> In them respectively, Empress Jungū and Emperor Suijin become ''kannushi''.<ref name="eos"> {{cite web | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | work= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | accessdate= 2009-10-16 }} </ref>

''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[Tokyo]]’s [[Kokugakuin University]] or [[Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[Women in Japan|Women]] can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>

The clothes they wear, for example the ''[[jōe]]'', the ''eboshi'' and the ''kariginu'' (see photos), do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by ''kannushi'' include a baton called ''[[Shaku (Japanese ritual baton)|shaku]]'' and a wand decorated with white paper streamers (''[[shide (Shinto)|shide]]'') called ''[[ōnusa]]''. ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[Tokyo]]'s [[Kokugakuin University]] or [[Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[Women in Japan|Women]] can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>

The clothes they wear, for example the ''[[jōe]]'', the ''eboshi'' and the ''kariginu'' (see photos), do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by ''kannushi'' include a baton called ''[[Shaku (Japanese ritual baton)|shaku]]'' and a wand decorated with white paper streamers (''[[shide (Shinto)|shide]]'') called ''[[ōnusa]]''. ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[Tokyo]]'s [[Kokugakuin University]] or [[Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[Women in Japan|Women]] can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>

''Kannushi'' can marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | accessdate= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
The clothes they wear, for example the ''[[jōe]]'', the ''eboshi'' and the ''kariginu'' (see photos), do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by ''kannushi'' include a baton called ''[[Shaku (Japanese ritual baton)|shaku]]'' and a wand decorated with white paper streamers (''[[shide (Shinto)|shide]]'') called ''[[ōnusa]]''. ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[Tokyo]]'s [[Kokugakuin University]] or [[Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[Women in Japan|Women]] can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>

{{redirect|Shinshoku|the [[L'Arc-en-Ciel]] song|Shinshoku (Lose Control)}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|thumb|left|A ''kannushi'' wearing a ''[[jōe]]'' being accompanied by two ''[[miko]]'']] [[File:Shinto_priest_in_full_dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|thumb|180px|right|A ''kannushi'' in full dress, [[Katori Shrine]]]] [[File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|thumb|180px|right|A ''kannushi'' wearing a garment called ''kariginu'' and a hat called eboshi]]
A {{nihongo3|"god master"|神主|'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced ''kamunushi''}}, also called {{nihongo|'''shinshoku'''|神職|extra=meaning "god's employee"}}, is a person responsible for the maintenance of a {{nihongo|[[Shinto]] [[shinto shrine|shrine]]|神社|''jinja''}} as well as for leading worship of a given ''[[kami]]''.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for ''kannushi'' are sometimes also read as '''''jinshu''''' with the same meaning.
Originally, the ''kannushi'' were intermediaries between ''kami'' and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A ''kannushi'' was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of [[Harae|purificatory rites]], was able to work as a medium for a [[kami]]. Later the term evolved to being synonymous with ''shinshoku'', that is, a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = Japanese |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |accessdate = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archiveurl = https://www.webcitation.org/6AfIob8e3?url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archivedate = 2012-09-14 }}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (680 AD) and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (720 AD).<ref name="ya"/> In them respectively, [[Empress Jingū]] and [[Emperor Sujin]] became ''kannushi''.<ref name="eos"> {{cite web | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | work= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | accessdate= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
Within the same shrine, for example at [[Ise Jingū]] or [[Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of ''kannushi'' at the same time called for example {{nihongo|''Ō-kannushi''|大神主}}, {{nihongo|''Sō-kannushi''|総神主}}, or {{nihongo|''Gon-kannushi''|権神主}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/>
''Kannushi'' can marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | accessdate= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
The clothes they wear, for example the ''[[jōe]]'', the ''eboshi'' and the ''kariginu'' (see photos), do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by ''kannushi'' include a baton called ''[[Shaku (Japanese ritual baton)|shaku]]'' and a wand decorated with white paper streamers (''[[shide (Shinto)|shide]]'') called ''[[ōnusa]]''. ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[Tokyo]]'s [[Kokugakuin University]] or [[Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[Women in Japan|Women]] can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>

Later the term evolved to being synonymous with ''shinshoku'', that is, a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = ja |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |access-date = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://www.webcitation.org/6AfIob8e3?url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archive-date = 2012-09-14 }}</ref>

The term appears in both the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (680 AD) and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (720 AD).<ref name="ya"/> In them respectively, [[Empress Jingū]] and [[Emperor Sujin]] became ''kannushi''.<ref name="eos"> {{cite web | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | work= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
Within the same shrine, for example at [[Ise Jingū]] or [[Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of ''kannushi'' at the same time called for example {{nihongo|''Ō-kannushi''|大神主}}, {{nihongo|''Sō-kannushi''|総神主}}, or {{nihongo|''Gon-kannushi''|権神主}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/>
''Kannushi'' can marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
The clothes they wear, for example the ''[[jōe]]'', the ''eboshi'' and the ''kariginu'' (see photos), do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by ''kannushi'' include a baton called ''[[Shaku (Japanese ritual baton)|shaku]]'' and a wand decorated with white paper streamers (''[[shide (Shinto)|shide]]'') called ''[[ōnusa]]''. ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[Tokyo]]'s [[Kokugakuin University]] or [[Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[Women in Japan|Women]] can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>

''Kannushi'' can marry and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
The clothes they wear, for example the ''[[jōe]]'', the ''eboshi'' and the ''kariginu'' (see photos), do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments used in the past by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between ''kami'' worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by ''kannushi'' include a baton called ''[[Shaku (Japanese ritual baton)|shaku]]'' and a wand decorated with white paper streamers (''[[shide (Shinto)|shide]]'') called ''[[ōnusa]]''. ''Kannushi'' are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called ''[[miko]]''.
To become a ''kannushi'', a novice must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[Tokyo]]'s [[Kokugakuin University]] or [[Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[Women in Japan|Women]] can also become ''kannushi'' and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine attendent}} {{redirect|Shinshoku|the [[L'Arc-en-Ciel]] song|Shinshoku (Lose Control)}} {{Italic title}} {{Shinto}} [[File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|thumb|left|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}]] [[File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|thumb|180px|left|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Shrine]]]] [[File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|thumb|180px|left|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat]]
A {{nihongo3|"god master"|[[wiktionary:神主|神主]]|'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced {{transl|ja|kamunushi}}}}, also called {{nihongo3|meaning "god's employee"|神職|'''shinshoku'''}}, is a person responsible for the maintenance of a {{nihongo|[[Shinto]] [[shinto shrine|shrine]]|神社|jinja}} as well as for leading worship of a given {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for {{transl|ja|kannushi}} are sometimes also read as '''{{transl|ja|jinshu}}''' with the same meaning.
Originally, the {{transl|ja|kannushi}} were intermediaries between {{transl|ja|kami}} and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of [[Harae|purificatory rites]], was able to work as a medium for a {{transl|ja|kami}}. Later the term evolved to being synonymous with {{transl|ja|shinshoku}} - a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = ja |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |access-date = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://www.webcitation.org/6AfIob8e3?url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archive-date = 2012-09-14 }}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} (680 AD) and {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} (720 AD),<ref name="ya"/> where the [[Empress Jingū]] and [[Emperor Sujin]] respectively become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"> {{cite web | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | work= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Jingū]] or [[Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/>
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|[[Shaku (Japanese ritual baton)|shaku]]}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|[[shide (Shinto)|shide]]}}) called {{transl|ja|[[ōnusa]]}}. {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a novice must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[Tokyo]]'s [[Kokugakuin University]] or [[Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[Women in Japan|Women]] can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== See also == * {{transl|ja|[[Miko]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Norito]]}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Kannushi]][[de:Shinshoku]][[en:Shinto priest]][[es:Kannushi]][[fa:کاننوشی]][[fi:Kannushi]][[fr:Kannushi]][[id:Kannushi]][[it:Kannushi]][[ja:神職]][[jv:Kannushi]][[ms:Kannushi]][[or:କାନ୍ନୁଶୀ]][[pl:Kannushi]][[ru:Каннуси]][[te:కన్నూషి]][[th:คันนูชิ]][[tr:Kannushi]][[uk:Каннусі]][[zh:神職 (神道)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q60523]] Wikidata name: kannushi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q60523]] Wikidata name: kannushi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Expand Japanese|神職|date=January 2024|topic=cult}}
{{Short description|Shinto shrine attendant}} {{redirect|Shinshoku|the [[:en:L'Arc-en-Ciel|L'Arc-en-Ciel]] song|Shinshoku (Lose Control)}} {{Italic title}} {{Shinto}}
A {{nihongo3|"divine [[:en:Master of ceremonies|master (of ceremonies)]]"||'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced {{transl|ja|kamunushi}}}}, also called {{nihongo3|meaning "employee/worker of kami"|神職|'''shinshoku'''}}, is a person responsible for the maintenance of a {{nihongo|[[Shinto]] [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]]|神社|jinja}} as well as for leading worship of a given {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for {{transl|ja|kannushi}} are sometimes also read as '''{{transl|ja|jinshu}}''' with the same meaning.
== History == Originally, the {{transl|ja|kannushi}} were intermediaries between {{transl|ja|kami}} and people and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of [[:en:Harae|purificatory rites]], was able to work as a medium for a {{transl|ja|kami}}. Later the term evolved to being synonymous with {{transl|ja|shinshoku}} - a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = ja |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |access-date = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://archive.today/20121209091437/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archive-date = 2012-12-09 }}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} (680 AD) and {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} (720 AD),<ref name="ya"/> where the [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] and [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] respectively become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"> {{cite encyclopedia | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | encyclopedia= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|[[:en:Hu (ritual baton)|shaku]]}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|[[:en:Shide (Shinto)|shide]]}}) called {{transl|ja|[[:en:Ōnusa|Ōnusa]]}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a [[:en:Novice|Novice]] must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]'s [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]] or [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[:en:Women in Japan|Women]] can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a [[:en:Deacon|Deacon]] or an [[:en:Acolyte|Acolyte]]. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|[[Miko]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Norito]]}}
== References == {{reflist}}
== External links == {{commons Category:11]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Kannushi]][[de:Shinshoku]][[en:Shinto priest]][[es:Kannushi]][[fa:کاننوشی]][[fi:Kannushi]][[fr:Kannushi]][[id:Kannushi]][[it:Kannushi]][[ja:神職]][[jv:Kannushi]][[ms:Kannushi]][[or:କାନ୍ନୁଶୀ]][[pl:Kannushi]][[ru:Каннуси]][[te:కన్నూషి]][[th:คันนูชิ]][[tr:Kannushi]][[uk:Каннусі]][[zh:神職 (神道)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q60523]] Wikidata name: kannushi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q60523]] Wikidata name: kannushi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Expand Japanese|神職|date=January 2024|topic=cult}}
{{Short description|Shinto shrine attendant}} {{redirect|Shinshoku|the [[:en:L'Arc-en-Ciel|L'Arc-en-Ciel]] song|Shinshoku (Lose Control)}} {{Italic title}} {{Shinto}}
A {{nihongo3|"divine [[:en:Master of ceremonies|master (of ceremonies)]]"||'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced {{transl|ja|kamunushi}}}}, also called {{nihongo3|meaning "employee/worker of kami"|神職|'''shinshoku'''}}, is a person responsible for the maintenance of a {{nihongo|[[Shinto]] [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]]|神社|jinja}} as well as for leading worship of a given {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for {{transl|ja|kannushi}} are sometimes also read as '''{{transl|ja|jinshu}}''' with the same meaning.
== History == Originally, the {{transl|ja|kannushi}} were intermediaries between {{transl|ja|kami}} and people and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of [[:en:Harae|purificatory rites]], was able to work as a medium for a {{transl|ja|kami}}. Later the term evolved to being synonymous with {{transl|ja|shinshoku}} - a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = ja |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |access-date = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://archive.today/20121209091437/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archive-date = 2012-12-09 }}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} (680 AD) and {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} (720 AD),<ref name="ya"/> where the [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] and [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] respectively become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"> {{cite encyclopedia | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | encyclopedia= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|[[:en:Hu (ritual baton)|shaku]]}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|[[:en:Shide (Shinto)|shide]]}}) called {{transl|ja|[[:en:Ōnusa|Ōnusa]]}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a [[:en:Novice|Novice]] must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]'s [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]] or [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[:en:Women in Japan|Women]] can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a [[:en:Deacon|Deacon]] or an [[:en:Acolyte|Acolyte]]. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|[[Miko]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Norito]]}}
