[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ca:Kannushi]] [[de:Shinshoku]] [[es:Kannushi]] [[fa:کاننوشی]] [[fi:Kannushi]] [[fr:Kannushi]] [[id:Kannushi]] [[it:Kannushi]] [[ja:神職]] [[jv:Kannushi]] [[ms:Kannushi]] [[or:କାନ୍ନୁଶୀ]] [[pl:Kannushi]] [[ru:Каннуси]] [[te:కన్నూషి]] [[th:คันนูชิ]] [[tr:Kannushi]] [[uk:Каннусі]] [[zh:神職 (神道)]] {{wikidata link|Q60523}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Kannushi]][[de:Shinshoku]][[en:Shinto priest]][[es:Kannushi]][[fa:کاننوشی]][[fi:Kannushi]][[fr:Kannushi]][[id:Kannushi]][[it:Kannushi]][[ja:神職]][[jv:Kannushi]][[ms:Kannushi]][[or:କାନ୍ନୁଶୀ]][[pl:Kannushi]][[ru:Каннуси]][[te:కన్నూషి]][[th:คันนูชิ]][[tr:Kannushi]][[uk:Каннусі]][[zh:神職 (神道)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q60523]] Wikidata name: kannushi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q60523]] Wikidata name: kannushi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Expand Japanese|神職|date=January 2024|topic=cult}}
{{Short description|Shinto shrine attendant}} {{redirect|Shinshoku|the [[:en:L'Arc-en-Ciel|L'Arc-en-Ciel]] song|Shinshoku (Lose Control)}} {{Italic title}} {{Shinto}}
A {{nihongo3|"divine [[:en:Master of ceremonies|master (of ceremonies)]]"||'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced {{transl|ja|kamunushi}}}}, also called {{nihongo3|meaning "employee/worker of kami"|神職|'''shinshoku'''}}, is a person responsible for the maintenance of a {{nihongo|[[Shinto]] [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]]|神社|jinja}} as well as for leading worship of a given {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for {{transl|ja|kannushi}} are sometimes also read as '''{{transl|ja|jinshu}}''' with the same meaning.
== History == Originally, the {{transl|ja|kannushi}} were intermediaries between {{transl|ja|kami}} and people and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of [[:en:Harae|purificatory rites]], was able to work as a medium for a {{transl|ja|kami}}. Later the term evolved to being synonymous with {{transl|ja|shinshoku}} - a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = ja |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |access-date = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://archive.today/20121209091437/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archive-date = 2012-12-09 }}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} (680 AD) and {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} (720 AD),<ref name="ya"/> where the [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] and [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] respectively become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"> {{cite encyclopedia | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | encyclopedia= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|[[:en:Hu (ritual baton)|shaku]]}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|[[:en:Shide (Shinto)|shide]]}}) called {{transl|ja|[[:en:Ōnusa|Ōnusa]]}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a [[:en:Novice|Novice]] must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]'s [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]] or [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[:en:Women in Japan|Women]] can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a [[:en:Deacon|Deacon]] or an [[:en:Acolyte|Acolyte]]. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|[[Miko]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Norito]]}}
== References == {{reflist}}
== External links == {{commons Category:11]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine attendant}} {{redirect|Shinshoku|the [[:en:L'Arc-en-Ciel|L'Arc-en-Ciel]] song|Shinshoku (Lose Control)}} {{Italic title}} {{Shinto}}
A {{nihongo3|"divine [[:en:Master of ceremonies|master (of ceremonies)]]"||'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced {{transl|ja|kamunushi}}}}, also called {{nihongo3|meaning "employee/worker of kami"|神職|'''shinshoku'''}}, is a person responsible for the maintenance of a {{nihongo|[[Shinto]] [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]]|神社|jinja}} as well as for leading worship of a given {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for {{transl|ja|kannushi}} are sometimes also read as '''{{transl|ja|jinshu}}''' with the same meaning.
== History == Originally, the {{transl|ja|kannushi}} were intermediaries between {{transl|ja|kami}} and people and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of [[:en:Harae|purificatory rites]], was able to work as a medium for a {{transl|ja|kami}}. Later the term evolved to being synonymous with {{transl|ja|shinshoku}} - a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = ja |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |access-date = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://archive.today/20121209091437/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archive-date = 2012-12-09 }}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} (680 AD) and {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} (720 AD),<ref name="ya"/> where the [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] and [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] respectively become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"> {{cite encyclopedia | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | encyclopedia= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|[[:en:Hu (ritual baton)|shaku]]}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|[[:en:Shide (Shinto)|shide]]}}) called {{transl|ja|[[:en:Ōnusa|Ōnusa]]}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a [[:en:Novice|Novice]] must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]'s [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]] or [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[:en:Women in Japan|Women]] can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a [[:en:Deacon|Deacon]] or an [[:en:Acolyte|Acolyte]]. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|[[Miko]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Norito]]}}

{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|[[:en:Hu (ritual baton)|shaku]]}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|[[:en:Shide (Shinto)|shide]]}}) called {{transl|ja|[[:en:Ōnusa|Ōnusa]]}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a [[:en:Novice|Novice]] must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]'s [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]] or [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[:en:Women in Japan|Women]] can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a [[:en:Deacon|Deacon]] or an [[:en:Acolyte|Acolyte]]. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|[[Miko]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Norito]]}}
== References == {{reflist}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine attendant}} {{redirect|Shinshoku|the [[:en:L'Arc-en-Ciel|L'Arc-en-Ciel]] song|Shinshoku (Lose Control)}} {{Italic title}} {{Shinto}}
A {{nihongo3|"divine [[:en:Master of ceremonies|master (of ceremonies)]]"||'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced {{transl|ja|kamunushi}}}}, also called {{nihongo3|meaning "employee/worker of kami"|神職|'''shinshoku'''}}, is a person responsible for the maintenance of a {{nihongo|[[Shinto]] [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]]|神社|jinja}} as well as for leading worship of a given {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for {{transl|ja|kannushi}} are sometimes also read as '''{{transl|ja|jinshu}}''' with the same meaning.
== History == Originally, the {{transl|ja|kannushi}} were intermediaries between {{transl|ja|kami}} and people and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of [[:en:Harae|purificatory rites]], was able to work as a medium for a {{transl|ja|kami}}. Later the term evolved to being synonymous with {{transl|ja|shinshoku}} - a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = ja |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |access-date = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://archive.today/20121209091437/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archive-date = 2012-12-09 }}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} (680 AD) and {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} (720 AD),<ref name="ya"/> where the [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] and [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] respectively become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"> {{cite encyclopedia | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | encyclopedia= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|[[:en:Hu (ritual baton)|shaku]]}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|[[:en:Shide (Shinto)|shide]]}}) called {{transl|ja|[[:en:Ōnusa|Ōnusa]]}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a [[:en:Novice|Novice]] must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]'s [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]] or [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[:en:Women in Japan|Women]] can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a [[:en:Deacon|Deacon]] or an [[:en:Acolyte|Acolyte]]. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine attendant}} {{redirect|Shinshoku|the [[:en:L'Arc-en-Ciel|L'Arc-en-Ciel]] song|Shinshoku (Lose Control)}} {{Italic title}} {{Shinto}}
A {{nihongo3|"divine [[:en:Master of ceremonies|master (of ceremonies)]]"||'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced {{transl|ja|kamunushi}}}}, also called {{nihongo3|meaning "employee/worker of kami"|神職|'''shinshoku'''}}, is a person responsible for the maintenance of a {{nihongo|[[Shinto]] [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]]|神社|jinja}} as well as for leading worship of a given {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for {{transl|ja|kannushi}} are sometimes also read as '''{{transl|ja|jinshu}}''' with the same meaning.
== History == Originally, the {{transl|ja|kannushi}} were intermediaries between {{transl|ja|kami}} and people and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of [[:en:Harae|purificatory rites]], was able to work as a medium for a {{transl|ja|kami}}. Later the term evolved to being synonymous with {{transl|ja|shinshoku}} - a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = ja |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |access-date = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://archive.today/20121209091437/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archive-date = 2012-12-09 }}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} (680 AD) and {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} (720 AD),<ref name="ya"/> where the [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] and [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] respectively become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"> {{cite encyclopedia | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | encyclopedia= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]], there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|[[:en:Hu (ritual baton)|shaku]]}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|[[:en:Shide (Shinto)|shide]]}}) called {{transl|ja|[[:en:Ōnusa|Ōnusa]]}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a [[:en:Novice|Novice]] must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]'s [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]] or [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise]]'s [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kogakkan University]], or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> [[:en:Women in Japan|Women]] can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a [[:en:Deacon|Deacon]] or an [[:en:Acolyte|Acolyte]]. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|[[Miko]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Norito]]}}

A {{nihongo3|"divine {{ill|Master of ceremonies|en|Master of ceremonies|WD=Q497240|lt=master (of ceremonies)}}"||'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced {{transl|ja|kamunushi}}}}, also called {{nihongo3|meaning "employee/worker of kami"|神職|'''shinshoku'''}}, is a person responsible for the maintenance of a {{nihongo|[[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}}|神社|jinja}} as well as for leading worship of a given {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien}}|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for {{transl|ja|kannushi}} are sometimes also read as '''{{transl|ja|jinshu}}''' with the same meaning.
== History == Originally, the {{transl|ja|kannushi}} were intermediaries between {{transl|ja|kami}} and people and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of {{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=purificatory rites}}, was able to work as a medium for a {{transl|ja|kami}}. Later the term evolved to being synonymous with {{transl|ja|shinshoku}} - a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = ja |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |access-date = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://archive.today/20121209091437/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archive-date = 2012-12-09 }}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} (680 AD) and {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} (720 AD),<ref name="ya"/> where the {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}} respectively become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"> {{cite encyclopedia | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | encyclopedia= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}}, there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Hu (ritual baton)|en|Hu (ritual baton)|WD=Q1185997|lt=shaku}}}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|{{ill|Shide (Shinto)|en|Shide (Shinto)|WD=Q2657982|lt=shide}}}}) called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Ōnusa|en|Ōnusa|WD=Q1153887|lt=Ōnusa}}}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a {{ill|Novice|en|Novice|WD=Q1183487|lt=Novice}} must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Jinja Honchō}}, typically {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}'s {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}} or {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}}'s {{ill|Kogakkan University|en|Kogakkan University|WD=Q1797584|lt=Kogakkan University}}, or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> {{ill|Women in Japan|en|Women in Japan|WD=Q8031453|lt=Women}} can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a {{ill|Deacon|en|Deacon|WD=Q161944|lt=Deacon}} or an {{ill|Acolyte|en|Acolyte|WD=Q420421|lt=Acolyte}}. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|[[Miko]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Norito]]}}

A {{nihongo3|"divine {{ill|Master of ceremonies|en|Master of ceremonies|WD=Q497240|lt=master (of ceremonies)}}"||'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced {{transl|ja|kamunushi}}}}, also called {{nihongo3|meaning "employee/worker of kami"|神職|'''shinshoku'''}}, is a person responsible for the maintenance of a {{nihongo|[[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}}|神社|jinja}} as well as for leading worship of a given {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien}}|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for {{transl|ja|kannushi}} are sometimes also read as '''{{transl|ja|jinshu}}''' with the same meaning.
== History == Originally, the {{transl|ja|kannushi}} were intermediaries between {{transl|ja|kami}} and people and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of {{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=purificatory rites}}, was able to work as a medium for a {{transl|ja|kami}}. Later the term evolved to being synonymous with {{transl|ja|shinshoku}} - a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = ja |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |access-date = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://archive.today/20121209091437/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archive-date = 2012-12-09 }}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} (680 AD) and {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} (720 AD),<ref name="ya"/> where the {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}} respectively become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"> {{cite encyclopedia | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | encyclopedia= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}}, there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Hu (ritual baton)|en|Hu (ritual baton)|WD=Q1185997|lt=shaku}}}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|{{ill|Shide (Shinto)|en|Shide (Shinto)|WD=Q2657982|lt=shide}}}}) called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Ōnusa|en|Ōnusa|WD=Q1153887|lt=Ōnusa}}}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a {{ill|Novice|en|Novice|WD=Q1183487|lt=Novice}} must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Association of Shinto Shrines}}, typically {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}'s {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}} or {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}}'s {{ill|Kogakkan University|en|Kogakkan University|WD=Q1797584|lt=Kogakkan University}}, or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> {{ill|Women in Japan|en|Women in Japan|WD=Q8031453|lt=Women}} can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a {{ill|Deacon|en|Deacon|WD=Q161944|lt=Deacon}} or an {{ill|Acolyte|en|Acolyte|WD=Q420421|lt=Acolyte}}. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.

== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}}, there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Hu (ritual baton)|en|Hu (ritual baton)|WD=Q1185997|lt=shaku}}}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|{{ill|Shide (Shinto)|en|Shide (Shinto)|WD=Q2657982|lt=shide}}}}) called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Ōnusa|en|Ōnusa|WD=Q1153887|lt=Ōnusa}}}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingū|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a {{ill|Novice|en|Novice|WD=Q1183487|lt=Novice}} must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Association of Shinto Shrines}}, typically {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}'s {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}} or {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}}'s {{ill|Kogakkan University|en|Kogakkan University|WD=Q1797584|lt=Kogakkan University}}, or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> {{ill|Women in Japan|en|Women in Japan|WD=Q8031453|lt=Women}} can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a {{ill|Deacon|en|Deacon|WD=Q161944|lt=Deacon}} or an {{ill|Acolyte|en|Acolyte|WD=Q420421|lt=Acolyte}}. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Kannushi]][[de:Shinshoku]][[en:Shinto priest]][[es:Kannushi]][[fa:کاننوشی]][[fi:Kannushi]][[fr:Kannushi]][[id:Kannushi]][[it:Kannushi]][[ja:神職]][[jv:Kannushi]][[ms:Kannushi]][[or:କାନ୍ନୁଶୀ]][[pl:Kannushi]][[ru:Каннуси]][[te:కన్నూషి]][[th:คันนูชิ]][[tr:Kannushi]][[uk:Каннусі]][[zh:神職 (神道)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Kannushi]][[de:Shinshoku]][[en:Shinto priest]][[es:Kannushi]][[fa:کاننوشی]][[fi:Kannushi]][[fr:Kannushi]][[id:Kannushi]][[it:Kannushi]][[ja:神職]][[jv:Kannushi]][[ms:Kannushi]][[or:କାନ୍ନୁଶୀ]][[pl:Kannushi]][[ru:Каннуси]][[te:కన్నూషి]][[th:คันนูชิ]][[tr:Kannushi]][[uk:Каннусі]][[zh:神職 (神道)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Kannushi]][[de:Shinshoku]][[en:Shinto priest]][[es:Kannushi]][[fa:کاننوشی]][[fi:Kannushi]][[fr:Kannushi]][[id:Kannushi]][[it:Kannushi]][[ja:神職]][[jv:Kannushi]][[ms:Kannushi]][[or:କାନ୍ନୁଶୀ]][[pl:Kannushi]][[ru:Каннуси]][[te:కన్నూషి]][[th:คันนูชิ]][[tr:Kannushi]][[uk:Каннусі]][[zh:神職 (神道)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Kannushi]][[de:Shinshoku]][[en:Shinto priest]][[es:Kannushi]][[fa:کاننوشی]][[fi:Kannushi]][[fr:Kannushi]][[id:Kannushi]][[it:Kannushi]][[ja:神職]][[jv:Kannushi]][[ms:Kannushi]][[or:କାନ୍ନୁଶୀ]][[pl:Kannushi]][[ru:Каннуси]][[te:కన్నూషి]][[th:คันนูชิ]][[tr:Kannushi]][[uk:Каннусі]][[zh:神職 (神道)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q60523]] Wikidata name: kannushi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q60523]] Wikidata name: kannushi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Expand Japanese|神職|date=January 2024|topic=cult}}
{{Short description|Shinto shrine attendant}} {{redirect|Shinshoku|the {{ill|L'Arc-en-Ciel|en|L'Arc-en-Ciel|WD=Q218866|lt=L'Arc-en-Ciel}} song|Shinshoku (Lose Control)}} {{Italic title}} {{Shinto}}
A {{nihongo3|"divine {{ill|Master of ceremonies|en|Master of ceremonies|WD=Q497240|lt=master (of ceremonies)}}"||'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced {{transl|ja|kamunushi}}}}, also called {{nihongo3|meaning "employee/worker of kami"|神職|'''shinshoku'''}}, is a person responsible for the maintenance of a {{nihongo|[[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}}|神社|jinja}} as well as for leading worship of a given {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien}}|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for {{transl|ja|kannushi}} are sometimes also read as '''{{transl|ja|jinshu}}''' with the same meaning.
== History == Originally, the {{transl|ja|kannushi}} were intermediaries between {{transl|ja|kami}} and people and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of {{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=purificatory rites}}, was able to work as a medium for a {{transl|ja|kami}}. Later the term evolved to being synonymous with {{transl|ja|shinshoku}} - a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = ja |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |access-date = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://archive.today/20121209091437/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archive-date = 2012-12-09 }}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} (680 AD) and {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} (720 AD),<ref name="ya"/> where the {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}} respectively become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"> {{cite encyclopedia | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | encyclopedia= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}}, there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Hu (ritual baton)|en|Hu (ritual baton)|WD=Q1185997|lt=shaku}}}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|{{ill|Shide (Shinto)|en|Shide (Shinto)|WD=Q2657982|lt=shide}}}}) called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Ōnusa|en|Ōnusa|WD=Q1153887|lt=Ōnusa}}}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingū|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a {{ill|Novice|en|Novice|WD=Q1183487|lt=Novice}} must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Association of Shinto Shrines}}, typically {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}'s {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}} or {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}}'s {{ill|Kogakkan University|en|Kogakkan University|WD=Q1797584|lt=Kogakkan University}}, or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> {{ill|Women in Japan|en|Women in Japan|WD=Q8031453|lt=Women}} can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a {{ill|Deacon|en|Deacon|WD=Q161944|lt=Deacon}} or an {{ill|Acolyte|en|Acolyte|WD=Q420421|lt=Acolyte}}. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.

{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingū|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine attendant}} {{redirect|Shinshoku|the {{ill|L'Arc-en-Ciel|en|L'Arc-en-Ciel|WD=Q218866|lt=L'Arc-en-Ciel}} song|Shinshoku (Lose Control)}} {{Italic title}} {{Shinto}}
A {{nihongo3|"divine {{ill|Master of ceremonies|en|Master of ceremonies|WD=Q497240|lt=master (of ceremonies)}}"||'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced {{transl|ja|kamunushi}}}}, also called {{nihongo3|meaning "employee/worker of kami"|神職|'''shinshoku'''}}, is a person responsible for the maintenance of a {{nihongo|[[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}}|神社|jinja}} as well as for leading worship of a given {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien}}|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for {{transl|ja|kannushi}} are sometimes also read as '''{{transl|ja|jinshu}}''' with the same meaning.
== History == Originally, the {{transl|ja|kannushi}} were intermediaries between {{transl|ja|kami}} and people and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of {{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=purificatory rites}}, was able to work as a medium for a {{transl|ja|kami}}. Later the term evolved to being synonymous with {{transl|ja|shinshoku}} - a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = ja |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |access-date = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://archive.today/20121209091437/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archive-date = 2012-12-09 }}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} (680 AD) and {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} (720 AD),<ref name="ya"/> where the {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}} respectively become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"> {{cite encyclopedia | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | encyclopedia= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}}, there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Hu (ritual baton)|en|Hu (ritual baton)|WD=Q1185997|lt=shaku}}}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|{{ill|Shide (Shinto)|en|Shide (Shinto)|WD=Q2657982|lt=shide}}}}) called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Ōnusa|en|Ōnusa|WD=Q1153887|lt=Ōnusa}}}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingū|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a {{ill|Novice|en|Novice|WD=Q1183487|lt=Novice}} must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Association of Shinto Shrines}}, typically {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}'s {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}} or {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}}'s {{ill|Kogakkan University|en|Kogakkan University|WD=Q1797584|lt=Kogakkan University}}, or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> {{ill|Women in Japan|en|Women in Japan|WD=Q8031453|lt=Women}} can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a {{ill|Deacon|en|Deacon|WD=Q161944|lt=Deacon}} or an {{ill|Acolyte|en|Acolyte|WD=Q420421|lt=Acolyte}}. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.

A {{nihongo3|"divine {{ill|Master of ceremonies|en|Master of ceremonies|WD=Q497240|lt=master (of ceremonies)}}"||'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced {{transl|ja|kamunushi}}}}, also called {{nihongo3|meaning "employee/worker of kami"|神職|'''shinshoku'''}}, is a person responsible for the maintenance of a {{nihongo|[[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}}|神社|jinja}} as well as for leading worship of a given {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien}}|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for {{transl|ja|kannushi}} are sometimes also read as '''{{transl|ja|jinshu}}''' with the same meaning.
== History == Originally, the {{transl|ja|kannushi}} were intermediaries between {{transl|ja|kami}} and people and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of {{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=purificatory rites}}, was able to work as a medium for a {{transl|ja|kami}}. Later the term evolved to being synonymous with {{transl|ja|shinshoku}} - a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = ja |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |access-date = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://archive.today/20121209091437/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archive-date = 2012-12-09 }}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} (680 AD) and {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} (720 AD),<ref name="ya"/> where the {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}} respectively become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"> {{cite encyclopedia | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | encyclopedia= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}}, there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Hu (ritual baton)|en|Hu (ritual baton)|WD=Q1185997|lt=shaku}}}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|{{ill|Shide (Shinto)|en|Shide (Shinto)|WD=Q2657982|lt=shide}}}}) called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Ōnusa|en|Ōnusa|WD=Q1153887|lt=Ōnusa}}}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingū|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a {{ill|Novice|en|Novice|WD=Q1183487|lt=Novice}} must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Association of Shinto Shrines}}, typically {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}'s {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}} or {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}}'s {{ill|Kogakkan University|en|Kogakkan University|WD=Q1797584|lt=Kogakkan University}}, or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> {{ill|Women in Japan|en|Women in Japan|WD=Q8031453|lt=Women}} can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a {{ill|Deacon|en|Deacon|WD=Q161944|lt=Deacon}} or an {{ill|Acolyte|en|Acolyte|WD=Q420421|lt=Acolyte}}. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine attendant}} {{redirect|Shinshoku|the {{ill|L'Arc-en-Ciel|en|L'Arc-en-Ciel|WD=Q218866|lt=L'Arc-en-Ciel}} song|Shinshoku (Lose Control)}} {{Italic title}} {{Shinto}}
A {{nihongo3|"divine {{ill|Master of ceremonies|en|Master of ceremonies|WD=Q497240|lt=master (of ceremonies)}}"||'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced {{transl|ja|kamunushi}}}}, also called {{nihongo3|meaning "employee/worker of kami"|神職|'''shinshoku'''}}, is a person responsible for the maintenance of a {{nihongo|[[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}}|神社|jinja}} as well as for leading worship of a given {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien}}|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for {{transl|ja|kannushi}} are sometimes also read as '''{{transl|ja|jinshu}}''' with the same meaning.
== History == Originally, the {{transl|ja|kannushi}} were intermediaries between {{transl|ja|kami}} and people and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of {{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=purificatory rites}}, was able to work as a medium for a {{transl|ja|kami}}. Later the term evolved to being synonymous with {{transl|ja|shinshoku}} - a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = ja |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |access-date = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://archive.today/20121209091437/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archive-date = 2012-12-09 }}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} (680 AD) and {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} (720 AD),<ref name="ya"/> where the {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}} respectively become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"> {{cite encyclopedia | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | encyclopedia= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}}, there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Hu (ritual baton)|en|Hu (ritual baton)|WD=Q1185997|lt=shaku}}}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|{{ill|Shide (Shinto)|en|Shide (Shinto)|WD=Q2657982|lt=shide}}}}) called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Ōnusa|en|Ōnusa|WD=Q1153887|lt=Ōnusa}}}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingū|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a {{ill|Novice|en|Novice|WD=Q1183487|lt=Novice}} must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Association of Shinto Shrines}}, typically {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}'s {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}} or {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}}'s {{ill|Kogakkan University|en|Kogakkan University|WD=Q1797584|lt=Kogakkan University}}, or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> {{ill|Women in Japan|en|Women in Japan|WD=Q8031453|lt=Women}} can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a {{ill|Deacon|en|Deacon|WD=Q161944|lt=Deacon}} or an {{ill|Acolyte|en|Acolyte|WD=Q420421|lt=Acolyte}}. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.

In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} (680 AD) and {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} (720 AD),<ref name="ya"/> where the {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}} respectively become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"> {{cite encyclopedia | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | encyclopedia= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}}, there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>

== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a {{ill|Novice|en|Novice|WD=Q1183487|lt=Novice}} must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Association of Shinto Shrines}}, typically {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}'s {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}} or {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}}'s {{ill|Kogakkan University|en|Kogakkan University|WD=Q1797584|lt=Kogakkan University}}, or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> {{ill|Women in Japan|en|Women in Japan|WD=Q8031453|lt=Women}} can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a {{ill|Deacon|en|Deacon|WD=Q161944|lt=Deacon}} or an {{ill|Acolyte|en|Acolyte|WD=Q420421|lt=Acolyte}}. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.

A {{nihongo3|"divine {{ill|Master of ceremonies|en|Master of ceremonies|WD=Q497240|lt=master (of ceremonies)}}"||'''kannushi'''|originally pronounced {{transl|ja|kamunushi}}}}, also called {{nihongo3|meaning "employee/worker of kami"|神職|'''shinshoku'''}}, is a person responsible for the maintenance of a {{nihongo|[[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}}|神社|jinja}} as well as for leading worship of a given {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}.<ref name="IK">* ''Kannushi'' (in Japanese), Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien}}|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The characters for {{transl|ja|kannushi}} are sometimes also read as '''{{transl|ja|jinshu}}''' with the same meaning.
== History == Originally, the {{transl|ja|kannushi}} were intermediaries between {{transl|ja|kami}} and people and could transmit their will to common humans.<ref name="eos"/> A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} was a man capable of miracles or a holy man who, because of his practice of {{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=purificatory rites}}, was able to work as a medium for a {{transl|ja|kami}}. Later the term evolved to being synonymous with {{transl|ja|shinshoku}} - a man who works at a shrine and holds religious ceremonies there.<ref name="IK"/><ref name="ya"> {{cite web |last = Moriyasu |first = Jin |title = Kannushi |language = ja |work = Nihon Hyakka Zensho |publisher = Shogakukan |url = http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |access-date = 2009-10-16 |url-status = dead |archive-url = https://archive.today/20121209091437/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E4%B8%BB/ |archive-date = 2012-12-09 }}</ref>
In ancient times, because of the overlap of political and religious power within a clan, it was the head of the clan who led the clansmen during religious functions, or else it could be another official.<ref name="ya"/> Later, the role evolved into a separate and more specialized form. The term appears in both the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} (680 AD) and {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} (720 AD),<ref name="ya"/> where the {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}} respectively become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"> {{cite encyclopedia | title= Kannushi | last= Nishimuta | first= Takao | date= 2007-03-28 | encyclopedia= Encyclopedia of Shinto | publisher= Kokugakuin | url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1285 | access-date= 2009-10-16}}</ref>
== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}}, there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Hu (ritual baton)|en|Hu (ritual baton)|WD=Q1185997|lt=shaku}}}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|{{ill|Shide (Shinto)|en|Shide (Shinto)|WD=Q2657982|lt=shide}}}}) called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Ōnusa|en|Ōnusa|WD=Q1153887|lt=Ōnusa}}}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingū|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a {{ill|Novice|en|Novice|WD=Q1183487|lt=Novice}} must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Association of Shinto Shrines}}, typically {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}'s {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}} or {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}}'s {{ill|Kogakkan University|en|Kogakkan University|WD=Q1797584|lt=Kogakkan University}}, or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> {{ill|Women in Japan|en|Women in Japan|WD=Q8031453|lt=Women}} can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a {{ill|Deacon|en|Deacon|WD=Q161944|lt=Deacon}} or an {{ill|Acolyte|en|Acolyte|WD=Q420421|lt=Acolyte}}. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|[[Miko]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Norito]]}}
== References == {{reflist}}
== External links == [[Category|Kannushi|lcfirst=yes}}

== Description == Within the same shrine, such as at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]] or {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}}, there can be different types of {{transl|ja|kannushi}} at the same time; these may be called, for example, {{nihongo||大神主|Ō-kannushi}}, {{nihongo||総神主|Sō-kannushi}}, or {{nihongo||権神主|Gon-kannushi}}.<ref name="eos"/><ref name="ya"/> {{transl|ja|Kannushi}} are assisted in their religious or clerical work by women called {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}}.
{{transl|ja|Kannushi}} can marry, and their children usually inherit their position.<ref name="nishi"> {{cite book | last= Nishimura | first= Hajime | title= A Comparative History of Ideas | publisher= Motilal Banarsidass | year= 1998 | isbn= 978-81-208-1004-4}}</ref> Although this hereditary status is no longer legally granted, it continues in practice.<ref name="EB"> {{cite web| title= Shinshoku | publisher= Encyclopædia Britannica Online | url= http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540847/shinshoku | access-date= 2009-10-16}} </ref>
=== Vestments and ritual objects === The clothes {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wear, such as the {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}}, the {{transl|ja|eboshi}} and {{transl|ja|kariginu}}, do not have any special religious significance, but are simply official garments previously used by the Imperial court.<ref name="nishi"/> This detail reveals the close connection between {{transl|ja|kami}} worship and the figure of the Emperor.<ref name="nishi"/> Other implements used by {{transl|ja|kannushi}} include a baton called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Hu (ritual baton)|en|Hu (ritual baton)|WD=Q1185997|lt=shaku|1=Hu (ritual baton)}}}} and a wand decorated with white paper streamers ({{transl|ja|{{ill|Shide (Shinto)|en|Shide (Shinto)|WD=Q2657982|lt=shide|1=Shide (Shinto)}}}}) called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Ōnusa|en|Ōnusa|WD=Q1153887|lt=Ōnusa|1=Ōnusa}}}}.
{{gallery |File:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|[[jōe]]}} being accompanied by two {{transl|ja|[[miko]]}} |File:Shinto priest in full dress,kannushi,katori-jingu-shrine,katori-city,japan.JPG|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} in full dress, [[Katori Jingū|Katori Shrine]] |File:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A {{transl|ja|kannushi}} wearing a {{transl|ja|kariginu}} and an {{transl|ja|eboshi}} hat }}
== Education == To become a {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, a {{ill|Novice|en|Novice|WD=Q1183487|lt=Novice|1=Novice}} must study at a university approved by the {{Nihongo|[[Association of Shinto Shrines]]|神社本庁|Association of Shinto Shrines}}, typically {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}'s {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University|1=Kokugakuin University}} or {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise|1=Ise, Mie}}'s {{ill|Kogakkan University|en|Kogakkan University|WD=Q1797584|lt=Kogakkan University|1=Kogakkan University}}, or pass an exam that will certify his qualification.<ref name="EB"/> {{ill|Women in Japan|en|Women in Japan|WD=Q8031453|lt=Women|1=Women in Japan}} can also become {{transl|ja|kannushi}}, and widows can succeed their husbands in their job.<ref name="EB"/>
== Onshi == An Onshi or Oshi (御師) is a lower level Shinto priest analogous to a {{ill|Deacon|en|Deacon|WD=Q161944|lt=Deacon|1=Deacon}} or an {{ill|Acolyte|en|Acolyte|WD=Q420421|lt=Acolyte|1=Acolyte}}. They are considered between a layperson and clergy.
